title: KL-Constrained Baum-Welch Under HMM Misspecification
summary:
This experiment empirically investigates KL-constrained Baum-Welch (KL-BW) vs standard Baum-Welch (BW) when fitting first-order HMMs to higher-order (k=2,3) Markov data — a canonical misspecification setting.

`method.py` implements: (1) synthetic k-th order HMM data generation (d=4 states, obs_dim=3, k_true∈{1,2,3}, T∈{100,500,2000}, 5 seeds, diagonal Gaussian emissions); (2) batched scaled forward-backward (Rabiner 1989) vectorized over all N sequences simultaneously for fast CPU execution; (3) standard BW with 5 random restarts; (4) KL-BW initialized from best BW, with geometric binary search for Lagrange multiplier λ satisfying KL(A_new[i,:]∥A_old[i,:])≤ε row-wise, for ε∈{0.01,0.05,0.1,0.5}; (5) Viterbi decoding accuracy with permutation matching; (6) parameter recovery (Frobenius error); (7) power-law bias-variance decomposition with bootstrap CIs; (8) paired t-test improvement metrics.

Results (45 configs, 4.6 min): k_true=1: BW=0.801, KL=0.824, +2.8%; k_true=2: BW=0.769, KL=0.766, -0.4%; k_true=3: BW=0.765, KL=0.780, +1.9%. Best ε=0.01 consistently. Statistical significance modest (p>0.05) due to seed variance, but individual runs show large gains (e.g. k=3,T=2000,seed=4: BW=0.699→KL=0.942). Four plots generated: accuracy vs T, bias-variance decomposition, parameter recovery, epsilon sensitivity.

Output schema: exp_gen_sol_out — 45 examples with predict_standard_bw, predict_kl_best, predict_kl_eps_* fields and metadata per configuration.
